---
title: "Selecting and Placing: gather, scatter, and the Indexing Model"
description: "gather is the operation everyone re-derives from the docs every single time. Here is the one sentence that makes it stop being mysterious, the intuition that it's a one-hot matmul without the matrix, and the mutation rule that separates a copy from a view ‚Äî where writing to the wrong one silently does nothing."
categories: [tensors]
date: 2026-08-18
depth: "Deep dive"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    code-fold: false
execute:
  freeze: auto
---

You have three search queries, and for each one, a model has scored six candidate documents. You want, for each query, the two best documents ‚Äî not their scores, which you already have, but the actual document *vectors*, pulled out and stacked so you can do something with them. Here is the setup:

::: {#3849321d .cell execution_count=1}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch
torch.manual_seed(0)

scores     = torch.randn(3, 6)        # 3 queries, a score for each of 6 candidates
candidates = torch.randn(3, 6, 4)     # 3 queries, 6 candidates, each a 4-dim vector
```
:::


The first half is easy ‚Äî `topk` hands you the top two scores *and where they were*:

::: {#766946e4 .cell execution_count=2}
``` {.python .cell-code}
top_scores, top_idx = torch.topk(scores, k=2, dim=-1)
print("top scores:", tuple(top_scores.shape), " their positions:", tuple(top_idx.shape))
print("query 0 picked candidates at positions:", top_idx[0].tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
top scores: (3, 2)  their positions: (3, 2)
query 0 picked candidates at positions: [3, 4]
```
:::
:::


```
top scores: (3, 2)  their positions: (3, 2)
query 0 picked candidates at positions: [3, 4]
```

Now the second half, which is the whole subject of this post: you have *indices* ‚Äî "query 0 wants candidates 4 and 1" ‚Äî and you need to use them to reach into `candidates` and pull out the right vectors. This is "selection by index," and it feels like it should be a one-liner, and it is, but which one-liner, and why it has the exact shape rules it has, is something most people look up every time they need it. By the end of this post you won't have to. The operation is `gather` (and its friendlier sibling `take_along_dim`), its inverse is `scatter`, and both come from a single idea that, once you see it, makes the shape rules obvious instead of arbitrary.

> **TL;DR** ‚Äî `gather` has one rule: the output takes the shape of the *index*, and the index replaces the coordinate **only along the chosen dimension** ‚Äî every other coordinate passes through unchanged. That's why the index must have the same rank as the input. Underneath, `gather` is a one-hot matrix multiply with the matrix elided, which is why it's fast and differentiable. Its inverse, `scatter` (and `scatter_add`), *writes* into indexed positions. And a trap that has nothing to do with either: advanced indexing returns a **copy** while slicing returns a **view**, so writing to a stored advanced-index result silently updates nothing.

## The one rule

Here is `gather`, and here is the only thing you need to memorize about it:

```
torch.gather(input, dim, index)

out[i, j, k] = input[ i, index[i, j, k], k ]        # for dim == 1
                     ~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~~
                     ONLY this coordinate is replaced by the index;
                     i and k are taken from the output position itself
```

Read that formula and every rule about `gather` falls out of it as a consequence rather than a fact to memorize:

- **The output has the shape of `index`.** Because `out[i,j,k]` is defined for exactly the positions `index` has.
- **`index` must have the same rank as `input`.** Because the formula needs an index value at every `(i, j, k)`, and `i` and `k` range over `input`'s other axes ‚Äî so `index` needs those axes too.
- **Every axis except `dim` passes through untouched.** The `i` and the `k` in `input[i, index[...], k]` come straight from the output coordinate; only the middle slot is redirected.

Now apply it to the retrieval problem. We have `candidates` of shape `(query, candidate, feature)` and we want to select along the **candidate** axis (dim 1), keeping query and feature. The index `top_idx` has shape `(query, 2)` ‚Äî but `gather` needs it to have the same rank as `candidates`, which is 3. So we add the missing feature axis and let it broadcast across all four features (the *same* candidate index applies to every feature of that candidate):

::: {#c8c35197 .cell execution_count=3}
``` {.python .cell-code}
picked = torch.gather(candidates, dim=1, index=top_idx.unsqueeze(-1).expand(-1, -1, 4))
print("picked shape:", tuple(picked.shape), " (query, top-k, feature)")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
picked shape: (3, 2, 4)  (query, top-k, feature)
```
:::
:::


```
picked shape: (3, 2, 4)  (query, top-k, feature)
```

That `unsqueeze(-1).expand(-1, -1, 4)` is the piece everyone fumbles, and it's fumbled precisely because the rank rule feels arbitrary until you've seen the formula. It isn't arbitrary: `gather` needs an index at every output position, the output has a feature axis, so the index needs one too ‚Äî and `expand` (stride-0, from the strides post) supplies it for free since the same candidate index governs all its features.

Because this specific pattern is so common, PyTorch gives you a version that does the `unsqueeze`/`expand` for you:

::: {#2ec6b13f .cell execution_count=4}
``` {.python .cell-code}
picked_easy = torch.take_along_dim(candidates, top_idx.unsqueeze(-1), dim=1)
print("take_along_dim matches gather?", torch.equal(picked_easy, picked))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
take_along_dim matches gather? True
```
:::
:::


```
take_along_dim matches gather? True
```

`take_along_dim` is the one I actually reach for ‚Äî it's `gather` with the boilerplate that causes the bugs handled internally. (NumPy users: this is exactly `np.take_along_axis`, and `np.put_along_axis` is the writing version we'll get to.) But it's worth being able to write the raw `gather`, because when a variant comes up that `take_along_dim` doesn't cover, you need to know what it's doing under the hood.

Let's prove all of this against an oracle ‚Äî the slow, obviously-correct loop that transcribes "for each query, for each of its top picks, copy that candidate's vector," which is the specification `gather` is a fast version of:

::: {#08211fc8 .cell execution_count=5}
``` {.python .cell-code}
def pick_reference(candidates, idx):
    """Oracle: literally copy candidates[q, idx[q,r]] for each query q, rank r."""
    Q, R = idx.shape
    out = torch.zeros(Q, R, candidates.shape[-1])
    for q in range(Q):
        for r in range(R):
            out[q, r] = candidates[q, idx[q, r]]
    return out

torch.testing.assert_close(picked, pick_reference(candidates, top_idx))
print("gather == the loop it replaces  ‚úì")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
gather == the loop it replaces  ‚úì
```
:::
:::


```
gather == the loop it replaces  ‚úì
```

## The intuition: gather is a one-hot matmul without the matrix

If the formula tells you *what* `gather` does, this tells you *why* it's the right primitive ‚Äî why it's fast, why it's differentiable, why it shows up everywhere. Selecting element `index[i]` from a vector is the same as taking a dot product with a one-hot vector that has its 1 at position `index[i]`:

::: {#e2ceaf4a .cell execution_count=6}
``` {.python .cell-code}
import torch.nn.functional as F

labels = torch.tensor([2, 0, 1, 2, 0])       # 5 items, each pointing at one of 3 slots
values = torch.tensor([1., 2., 3., 4., 5.])

# "select the value at each label" two ways:
via_index = values                            # here labels index INTO a table; see below
onehot    = F.one_hot(labels, num_classes=3).float()
print("one-hot rows for labels", labels.tolist(), ":")
print(onehot.int())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
one-hot rows for labels [2, 0, 1, 2, 0] :
tensor([[0, 0, 1],
        [1, 0, 0],
        [0, 1, 0],
        [0, 0, 1],
        [1, 0, 0]], dtype=torch.int32)
```
:::
:::


```
one-hot rows for labels [2, 0, 1, 2, 0] :
tensor([[0, 0, 1],
        [1, 0, 0],
        [0, 1, 0],
        [0, 0, 1],
        [1, 0, 0]])
```

Multiply a one-hot matrix by a table and you *select* rows of that table; multiply its transpose by a vector and you *scatter-add* that vector's entries into bins. Selection and its inverse are both matrix multiplies by a one-hot matrix ‚Äî and `gather`/`scatter` are simply those two matmuls with the (enormous, mostly-zero) one-hot matrix never actually built:

::: {#4065062d .cell execution_count=7}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# scatter-add ‚Äî sum each item's value into the bin its label names ‚Äî IS one-hot^T @ values
via_matmul = onehot.T @ values
via_scatter = torch.zeros(3).scatter_add_(0, labels, values)
print("one-hot·µÄ @ values :", via_matmul.tolist())
print("scatter_add       :", via_scatter.tolist())
print("same?", torch.allclose(via_matmul, via_scatter))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
one-hot·µÄ @ values : [7.0, 3.0, 5.0]
scatter_add       : [7.0, 3.0, 5.0]
same? True
```
:::
:::


```
one-hot·µÄ @ values : [7.0, 3.0, 5.0]
scatter_add       : [7.0, 3.0, 5.0]
same?             True
```

Label 0 collected items 0 and 4 (`2 + 5 = 7`), label 1 got item 2 (`3`), label 2 got items 0 and 3 (`1 + 4 = 5`). This is the single most useful thing to carry from this post: **`gather` selects, `scatter_add` bins, and both are one-hot matmuls with the matrix elided** ‚Äî which is why they're `O(n)` instead of `O(n¬∑classes)`, why gradients flow through them cleanly (a matmul is differentiable), and why the same two operations power embedding lookups, mixture-of-experts routing, and every "sum by group" aggregation you'll ever write.

## The trap that has nothing to do with gather: copies versus views

Before scatter, a detour into a bug you *will* hit, because it lives in the indexing syntax itself and it's silent. Recall from the strides post that a basic slice returns a **view** (shares memory), while indexing with a list or tensor ‚Äî "advanced indexing" ‚Äî returns a **copy**. That distinction is harmless for reads. For *writes*, it's a trap, and the trap has three separate questions hiding inside it that people conflate.

**Question 1: what does a read return ‚Äî a view or a copy?**

::: {#f2f95790 .cell execution_count=8}
``` {.python .cell-code}
t = torch.arange(10.)
sliced   = t[2:5]         # basic slice     -> VIEW (shares t's memory)
advanced = t[[2, 3, 4]]   # list index      -> COPY

sliced[0]   = 999.        # write into the view
advanced[1] = -999.       # write into the copy

print("t =", t.tolist())
print("writing to the slice reached t?         ", 999.  in t.tolist())
print("writing to the stored advanced index?   ", -999. in t.tolist(), " <- went nowhere")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
t = [0.0, 1.0, 999.0, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0]
writing to the slice reached t?          True
writing to the stored advanced index?    False  <- went nowhere
```
:::
:::


```
t = [0.0, 1.0, 999.0, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0]
writing to the slice reached t?          True
writing to the stored advanced index?    False  <- went nowhere
```

That's the slogan everyone learns, and for reads it's the end of the story. But watch what happens when the indexing sits on the *left* of an assignment.

**Question 2: does assignment *through* the index write back? Yes ‚Äî even `+=`.**

::: {#4d4a99df .cell execution_count=9}
``` {.python .cell-code}
t2 = torch.arange(10.)
t2[[2, 3, 4]] += 1                         # augmented assignment THROUGH the index
print("t2[[2,3,4]] += 1 :", t2.tolist())

t3 = torch.arange(10.)
t3[[2, 3, 4]] = torch.tensor([-1., -2., -3.])
print("t3[[2,3,4]] = v  :", t3.tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
t2[[2,3,4]] += 1 : [0.0, 1.0, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0]
t3[[2,3,4]] = v  : [0.0, 1.0, -1.0, -2.0, -3.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0]
```
:::
:::


```
t2[[2,3,4]] += 1 : [0.0, 1.0, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0]
t3[[2,3,4]] = v  : [0.0, 1.0, -1.0, -2.0, -3.0, 5.0, 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, 9.0]
```

`t2[[2,3,4]] += 1` **did** update `t2`, even though `t2[[2,3,4]]` on its own returns a copy. The reason is that Python turns `x[idx] += 1` into `x.__setitem__(idx, x.__getitem__(idx) + 1)` ‚Äî a read (producing the throwaway copy), an add, and then a *write-back through `__setitem__`*, which is an indexed put. The copy is scratch; the write lands.

So the rule that actually holds is not "advanced indexing doesn't write." It's: **assignment through the subscript writes; mutating a value you read out and stored does not.** `emb[ids] = new` updates `emb`. `rows = emb[ids]; rows[0] = new` updates nothing ‚Äî `rows` is a detached copy. I have watched this exact confusion cost someone a morning: a training loop that "reset" some embedding rows by pulling them out, zeroing the pulled-out copy, and wondering why the embeddings never changed.

**Question 3: what happens when indices collide?**

::: {#e33d8ab3 .cell execution_count=10}
``` {.python .cell-code}
dst   = torch.zeros(3)
dup   = torch.tensor([0, 0])        # position 0, twice
delta = torch.tensor([1., 2.])

dst[dup] += delta
print("dst[dup] += [1,2] :", dst.tolist(), " <- ONE write wins, not a sum")

dst2 = torch.zeros(3)
dst2.index_add_(0, dup, delta)
print("index_add_(0,dup) :", dst2.tolist(), " <- accumulates: 1 + 2 = 3")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
dst[dup] += [1,2] : [2.0, 0.0, 0.0]  <- ONE write wins, not a sum
index_add_(0,dup) : [3.0, 0.0, 0.0]  <- accumulates: 1 + 2 = 3
```
:::
:::


```
dst[dup] += [1,2] : [2.0, 0.0, 0.0]  <- ONE write wins, not a sum
index_add_(0,dup) : [3.0, 0.0, 0.0]  <- accumulates: 1 + 2 = 3
```

With duplicate indices, `dst[dup] += delta` computes both increments against the *original* value and writes them to the same slot, so one overwrites the other ‚Äî and *which* one is officially unspecified (and nondeterministic on GPU). If you mean "accumulate," you have to say so with `index_add_` or `scatter_add_`. The collision policy is part of your program's contract; `+=` leaves it implicit and therefore wrong.

Three questions ‚Äî *read returns what? assignment writes back? collisions accumulate?* ‚Äî with three different answers, and conflating them is how the "advanced indexing is a copy" slogan turns into a real bug.

## scatter: gather run backwards

With the one-hot-matmul picture in hand, `scatter` needs almost no new machinery ‚Äî it's `gather`'s inverse, *writing* values into indexed positions instead of reading them out. Same one rule, mirrored: for `dim=1`, `self[i, index[i,j], j] = src[i,j]`. Two uses cover almost everything you'll do with it:

::: {#ffbd5660 .cell execution_count=11}
``` {.python .cell-code}
labels = torch.tensor([2, 0, 1, 2])

# 1. one-hot encoding: place a 1 at each label's column
onehot = torch.zeros(4, 3).scatter_(1, labels.unsqueeze(1), 1.0)
print("one-hot:", onehot.int().tolist())
print("matches F.one_hot?", torch.equal(onehot, F.one_hot(labels, 3).float()))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
one-hot: [[0, 0, 1], [1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 1]]
matches F.one_hot? True
```
:::
:::


```
one-hot: [[0, 0, 1], [1, 0, 0], [0, 1, 0], [0, 0, 1]]
matches F.one_hot? True
```

::: {#2cad1335 .cell execution_count=12}
``` {.python .cell-code}
# 2. scatter_add: the binning operation from the intuition section
counts = torch.zeros(3).scatter_add_(0, labels, torch.ones(4))
print("count per label:", counts.tolist(), "(label2 appears twice)")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
count per label: [1.0, 1.0, 2.0] (label2 appears twice)
```
:::
:::


```
count per label: [1.0, 1.0, 2.0]
```

`scatter_add_` is the one to remember, because it's how you aggregate variable-sized groups with no loop: mixture-of-experts combining expert outputs, graph neural networks summing over each node's neighbours, any "reduce by key." (NumPy's equivalent is the slightly awkward `np.add.at(dst, idx, src)`, or `np.bincount` for the counting special case ‚Äî worth knowing if you cross between the libraries.)

## A coordinate grid, and the argument that transposes it

One more indexing tool, because you'll need it constantly and it has a trap of its own. To build a grid of coordinates ‚Äî for positional encodings, for sampling, for any "where is each pixel" computation ‚Äî you use `meshgrid`, and its one argument decides whether your coordinate system is right-side up:

::: {#65525c46 .cell execution_count=13}
``` {.python .cell-code}
H, W = 3, 5
ii, jj = torch.meshgrid(torch.arange(H), torch.arange(W), indexing='ij')
grid = torch.stack([ii, jj], dim=-1)
print("indexing='ij' -> shape", tuple(grid.shape), "| grid[1,3] =", grid[1, 3].tolist())
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
indexing='ij' -> shape (3, 5, 2) | grid[1,3] = [1, 3]
```
:::
:::


```
indexing='ij' -> shape (3, 5, 2) | grid[1,3] = [1, 3]
```

`'ij'` is the matrix convention: the first output index runs down rows, so `grid[1, 3]` is genuinely row 1, column 3. Pass `indexing='xy'` instead and the first two axes swap ‚Äî you'd get shape `(5, 3, 2)` and a silently transposed coordinate system. PyTorch has *warned* on the omitted argument since version 1.10 (the current default is `'ij'`, and the warning text says it will be required in a future release), so pass it always, as though it already were ‚Äî coordinate convention is part of your function's contract, and the years of transposed-grid bugs that motivated the warning all came from letting the default decide.

There's a sting in the tail worth knowing: `F.grid_sample`, the op you're most likely to *build* a grid for, wants its coordinates in **`(x, y)` order normalized to `[-1, 1]`** ‚Äî the opposite convention from the `(row, col)` grid you just naturally constructed. So the two functions you'd use together disagree, which is a rich source of upside-down outputs.

And, tying back to the strides post: `meshgrid` is just sugar over two broadcast `arange`s, which is what makes it stop being magic ‚Äî

::: {#f66da804 .cell execution_count=14}
``` {.python .cell-code}
i = torch.arange(H).view(H, 1).expand(H, W)   # row index, broadcast across columns
j = torch.arange(W).view(1, W).expand(H, W)   # column index, broadcast down rows
print("pure broadcast == meshgrid('ij')?", torch.equal(torch.stack([i, j], -1), grid))
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}
```
pure broadcast == meshgrid('ij')? True
```
:::
:::


```
pure broadcast == meshgrid('ij')? True
```

## What to carry away

Selection-by-index looks like a grab-bag of operations you memorize separately. It isn't ‚Äî it's two operations and their shared idea:

- **`gather` reads, `scatter` writes**, and both obey one rule: the output (or the write) has the shape of the *index*, which replaces the coordinate along one axis while every other coordinate passes through ‚Äî hence the same-rank requirement that trips everyone up.
- Underneath, both are **one-hot matrix multiplies with the matrix elided** ‚Äî which is the intuition that explains why they're fast, differentiable, and everywhere, and why `scatter_add` is the natural "sum by group."
- The **copy-versus-view** distinction is a separate trap: assignment *through* an index writes back (including `+=`), but mutating a value you read out and stored does not ‚Äî and duplicate indices need an explicit `index_add_` if you mean to accumulate.

If one sentence survives: *`gather` and `scatter` are how you select and place by index, they're one-hot matmuls in disguise, and the shape rules that seem arbitrary are exactly what that disguise requires.*

## Where this goes next

We've now moved data around by shape (strides), combined it across axes (broadcasting), and selected it by index (here) ‚Äî all of it exact, none of it worrying about the actual numbers involved. The next post is where the numbers bite back: floating-point arithmetic is not real arithmetic, and the textbook formula for softmax, for a distance, for a masked sum can be provably correct on paper and catastrophically wrong in `fp32`.

## References

- [`torch.gather`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.gather.html) ‚Äî read the formula in the docs once, out loud; it is the entire operation.
- [`torch.take_along_dim`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.take_along_dim.html) ‚Äî `gather` with the `unsqueeze`/`expand` boilerplate handled. Underused; reach for it first.
- [`torch.Tensor.scatter_add_`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.Tensor.scatter_add_.html) and [`torch.Tensor.index_add_`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.Tensor.index_add_.html) ‚Äî the explicit collision-accumulation policies.
- [`torch.meshgrid`](https://pytorch.org/docs/stable/generated/torch.meshgrid.html) ‚Äî and the `indexing=` argument you should always pass.
- The basic-vs-advanced indexing view/copy rule is inherited from NumPy; the [NumPy indexing docs](https://numpy.org/doc/stable/user/basics.indexing.html) explain it more directly than PyTorch's, and `np.take_along_axis` / `np.put_along_axis` / `np.add.at` are the direct analogues of the operations here.
